title: Finding Purpose
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Finding Purpose
summary: Scripture grounds purpose not in self-discovery but in divine design — we were created for good works prepared in advance, and our calling unfolds through faithfulness more than feelings.
body:
The search for purpose is universal, but Scripture reframes the question. The issue is not 'What do I want to do?' but 'What was I made for?' Paul tells the Ephesians they are God's workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works that God prepared in advance [verse:eph.2.10]. Purpose is not invented; it is discovered.

Jeremiah received one of Scripture's most direct statements about divine purpose: before I formed you in the womb I knew you, and before you were born I set you apart [verse:jer.1.5]. While this was spoken to a prophet, the principle that God acts with intention before birth echoes throughout Scripture.

Jesus defined his own purpose with clarity: the Son of Man came to seek and save the lost [verse:luke.19.10]. For believers, purpose flows from this same mission — love God, love neighbor, and make disciples [verse:matt.22.37-39].

Proverbs counsels patience in the process: a person's heart plans their way, but the Lord directs their steps [verse:prov.16.9]. Purpose unfolds through faithfulness in small things before it expands into larger ones [verse:luke.16.10].

Purpose in Scripture is less a destination to find and more a direction to walk — faithfully, daily, attentive to the God who goes before.

verses.annotation: God's workmanship — created for good works prepared beforehand.
Known and set apart before birth — divine intentionality.
Jesus defines his purpose: seeking and saving the lost.
The greatest commandments — love as the foundation of all purpose.
We plan, but God directs — purpose unfolds through surrender.
Faithful in little, faithful in much — purpose scales with stewardship.

scholars.quote: Schreiner argues that Ephesians 2:10 places human purpose squarely in divine initiative — the 'good works prepared in advance' means purpose is not something we generate but something we step into.
scholars.source: Paul, Apostle of God's Glory in Christ (Schreiner, 2001)

scholars.quote: Lundbom notes that Jeremiah 1:5's language of being 'known' before birth uses intimate relational vocabulary — God's purpose for Jeremiah was rooted in personal relationship, not abstract assignment.
Longman reads Proverbs 16:9 as wisdom's corrective to human autonomy — planning is wise, but assuming control over outcomes is folly. Purpose emerges in the tension between initiative and surrender.
